publicationStmt.p: Transcribed from Bodleian Library, Lovelace Byron collection, Box 166, folios 29-32. Transcription by Lisa Gold (2022). TEI conversion for Sextant pipeline, 2026.

div.p: Dearest W. Your long letter of Monday, received this morning, is delightful.
I have much to tell you since yesterday. My visit to Brampton was very agreeable. I was glad to renew my acquaintance with Lady Mary Hewett; who married I think the
same day as myself; & I have not seen her for many years. She has a fine boy a year younger than Byron.--
I had a very long & interesting, & I hope useful, conversation with Dr Jones about my mother, which I am very much pleased with.
But now I must explain arrangements. When I returned here, (with Annabella Acheson), in the

evening, my mother said she had seem some coaches pass thro' from Bedford to Cambridge; & that she advised me to go by one of them today, in preference to any other mode of going; as being both most convenient & cheapest. So this morning I walked over to St Neots, (a mile & 1/2 off) to get some things for the Hen; & then I took my place for 1/2 past 4 o'clock; for Mrs King; I have now just packed

my things into a Carpet- -Bag, & sent it off to the St Neots Inn by my mother's servant; explaining to him that I am in-cog, of course; & that he is not to speak of Mylady. I am well pleased with this arrangement.
I enclose you a letter received from Hester this morning, & from which you will perceive that I have an additional reason for wishing to go to Cambridge now in Mr Gamlen. This is to
me quite an unexpected delight. How I shall enjoy being there.

I would not leave my mother however, unless under the best care. She goes to Brampton this afternoon, where the Jones's & Achesons will be delightful for her. How long she will remain there, is quite uncertain; & consequently my movements likewise, which of course depend on her. I think she contemplates some
little alteration of her original plans; but as nothing is yet fixed, I say noo more on this subject today.
I am certainly a cormfort to her. She says that my activity, gentleness, & fun, are delightful. (Tho' this report ought not to come to you from myself).
I send you also a letter received from F. Knight this morning. I shall write to him
I long to get him to Ockham. Tho' of this he makes no mention; & indeed seems to be going quite in other directions.
I must remind the old Knight also about coming to Ockham.--
I am pleased that you admire Miss Jane. She is I think beautiful; at least will be when in condition. And I am assured she will do lots of work.
